response: The EU’s GDPR, in force since May 2018, applies to any company processing EU residents’ data and gives people rights such as access, erasure, portability, and explicit-consent protections. It also requires Data Protection Officers for large-scale processing, mandates breach reporting within 72 hours, and allows fines of up to 4% of global annual revenue or 20 million euros, whichever is higher. Major penalties include Meta’s 1.2 billion euro fine in 2023, Amazon’s 746 million euro fine, and Google’s fines totaling over 200 million euros. While critics say GDPR can burden small businesses and slow innovation, it has become the global benchmark for data privacy laws and established privacy as a fundamental right.
